enrichment.short_description: Habitica is a free habit-building and productivity app that turns your real-life tasks into a role-playing game, rewarding follow-through with experience, gold and gear.
enrichment.seo_title: Habitica Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Habitica turns your to-do list into an RPG. Read our 2026 review of features, pricing, pros and cons, and how it compares to other habit trackers.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Habitica is a free, open source habit-building and productivity application that applies role-playing game mechanics to everyday task management. Users create an avatar and log three types of real-life responsibilities, Habits (repeatable actions with positive or negative outcomes), Dailies (recurring tasks tied to a schedule), and To-Dos (one-off tasks), and the app converts consistent follow-through into experience points, in-game gold, and collectible equipment, while missed dailies cause the avatar to lose health points. The core idea is to exploit the same reward loops that make video games compelling and redirect them toward real-world habit formation, task completion, and goal tracking.

The project began in 2012 when web developer Tyler Renelle built a personal tool, inspired by habit-formation books such as The Power of Habit and The Now Habit, to help himself stick to routines. After a Lifehacker article introduced the tool, then called HabitRPG, to a wider audience and pushed signups from a handful of users to over 20,000 almost overnight, Renelle brought on Siena Leslie and Vicky Hsu as co-founders, and the trio formally incorporated HabitRPG, Inc. in 2014. On July 31, 2015 the product and company were renamed Habitica, referencing the fictional land in which players' avatars adventure. The company remains privately held and headquartered in Santa Monica, California, and the underlying codebase has been open source from early on, hosted publicly on GitHub with substantial community contribution.

Habitica is built as a web application with companion native apps for iOS and Android, all syncing to the same account so a task checked off on a phone updates the web dashboard instantly. Because it is open source, developers can inspect, self-host, or extend the underlying code, and the project exposes a public API that has spawned third-party integrations, browser extensions, and community tools. The system layers game mechanics, classes such as Warrior, Mage, Healer and Rogue, equipment, pets, mounts, and quests, on top of the task-tracking core, and social structures called Parties (small accountability groups that can battle bosses together) and Guilds (topic- or interest-based communities) extend the gamification into a social layer.

The task system separates Habits, Dailies and To-Dos so different types of behavior get modeled differently rather than forced into one generic list. Completing tasks earns gold, experience, and random item drops; missing scheduled Dailies costs the avatar health points, which is designed to create light stakes without punishing real-world failure too harshly. Beyond the core loop, Habitica offers avatar customization (hairstyles, skin tones, outfits, and accessibility options like wheelchairs), collectible pets and mounts earned through gameplay, Challenges that let users or organizations publish shared task lists other players can join, and a rewards column where users redeem earned gold for custom real-life rewards they define themselves, plus reminders, streak counters, and light and dark theming.

Habitica's core experience is entirely free with no feature paywall on the fundamental task-and-gamification loop. An optional paid subscription costs $5 per month, purchasable in 1-, 3-, 6-, or 12-month blocks, with the 12-month block discounted to roughly $4 per month, or $48 per year. Subscribing raises the monthly cap on purchasable Gems, the premium in-game currency used for cosmetic items and avatar customization, up to a maximum of 50 Gems per month, doubles the daily drop rate for in-game rewards, and unlocks subscriber-exclusive monthly mystery items and a unique pet. Notably, Habitica is explicitly not pay-to-win: non-subscribers can progress through the same content and mechanics as subscribers, with the subscription functioning more as a way to support development and unlock cosmetic and convenience perks.

Habitica's audience skews toward people who respond well to gamified accountability, students managing coursework and routines, people building health or productivity habits, ADHD and neurodivergent users who benefit from externalized structure and immediate feedback, and gaming-culture-adjacent users who find generic to-do apps unmotivating. Because Parties and Guilds add social accountability, it also appeals to friend groups, support communities, and online communities that use shared Challenges to keep members on track together.

Within the productivity and habit-tracking category, Habitica differentiates itself from mainstream to-do apps like Todoist and TickTick and dedicated habit trackers like Streaks by fully committing to an RPG framing rather than treating gamification as a bolt-on feature. Its closest peers are other game-like productivity tools, but Habitica's decade-plus track record, large community-run guild ecosystem, and fully open source codebase give it durability and transparency that many smaller competitors lack. The tradeoff is that its RPG-heavy interface has a steeper learning curve and more visual complexity than minimalist task managers, a deliberate positioning choice rather than an oversight.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Habitica is a free, open source productivity app that reframes habit-building and task management as a role-playing game. Instead of a plain checklist, users maintain an avatar that gains experience, gold and gear for real-life tasks completed, and loses health for missed recurring commitments.

Originally launched in 2012 as HabitRPG by developer Tyler Renelle, the project was renamed Habitica in 2015 after incorporating as a company in 2014. It remains privately held and headquartered in Santa Monica, California, with an open source codebase maintained on GitHub.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Habitica splits tasks into Habits, Dailies and To-Dos, each modeled differently so recurring routines, one-off errands and repeatable behaviors are tracked in ways that fit how they actually work in real life. Completing tasks earns gold, experience and item drops that feed into avatar progression, classes, equipment, pets and mounts.

Social features include Parties, small groups that team up to battle bosses through completed tasks, and Guilds, topic-based communities that support Challenges, shared task lists members can opt into together. A public API, cross-device sync, and customizable avatars round out the feature set.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Habitica's core app is entirely free, with no feature paywall on the main task and gamification loop. An optional $5 per month subscription, discounted to about $4 per month on annual billing, raises the monthly Gem purchase cap, doubles daily item drops, and unlocks subscriber-exclusive cosmetics.

Habitica is explicitly designed to not be pay-to-win: subscribers do not progress faster through content than free users, so the subscription functions mainly as a way to support development and access convenience and cosmetic perks.

features.title: Habits, Dailies and To-Dos
features.description: Three distinct task types model repeatable behaviors, scheduled routines and one-off items differently rather than forcing everything into a single generic list.

features.title: RPG avatar progression
features.description: Completing tasks earns gold, experience and item drops that level up an avatar with classes, gear, pets and mounts.

features.title: Parties
features.description: Small accountability groups of friends team up to battle in-game bosses through completed real-life tasks.

features.title: Guilds and Challenges
features.description: Topic-based communities let members share task lists and hold each other accountable through joinable Challenges.

features.title: Custom rewards column
features.description: Users define their own real-life rewards that can be purchased with in-game gold earned from completed tasks.

features.title: Cross-platform sync
features.description: Native iOS and Android apps sync instantly with the web app so progress stays consistent across devices.

features.title: Open API
features.description: A public API lets developers build integrations, browser extensions and community tools on top of Habitica.

features.title: Avatar customization
features.description: Hairstyles, skin tones, outfits and accessibility options like wheelchairs let users personalize their in-game character.

pros: Core app is entirely free with no feature paywall
Strong social accountability through Parties and Guilds
Fully open source and transparent development
Available on web, iOS and Android with instant sync
Explicitly not pay-to-win, so free users are not disadvantaged
cons: RPG-style interface has a learning curve compared to plain to-do apps
Gamified framing can feel too playful for users who want a minimalist tool
Losing health points for missed Dailies can add stress for some users
Reminder and notification features are less robust than dedicated task apps
In-game currency system (gold versus Gems) can be confusing at first

faqs.question: Is Habitica free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, Habitica's core habit tracker, to-do list, and RPG gamification system are entirely free. An optional $5 per month subscription unlocks extra Gems, faster item drops and subscriber-exclusive cosmetics.

faqs.question: What is Habitica used for?
faqs.answer: Habitica is used for building habits, tracking recurring routines, and managing to-do lists by turning task completion into RPG-style progression with experience points, gold and collectible items.

faqs.question: Is Habitica open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, Habitica's codebase has been open source since its early days and is hosted publicly on GitHub, allowing community contributions and transparency into how the app works.

faqs.question: What happens if I miss a Daily task in Habitica?
faqs.answer: Missing a scheduled Daily causes your avatar to lose health points. If health reaches zero, the avatar suffers a small in-game penalty, but there is no real-world consequence beyond that.

faqs.question: Does Habitica have a mobile app?
faqs.answer: Yes, Habitica has official native apps for both iOS and Android that sync in real time with the web version.

faqs.question: Can I use Habitica with friends?
faqs.answer: Yes, Habitica's Party feature lets small groups of friends team up to battle in-game bosses through completed tasks, and Guilds let users join larger interest-based communities and Challenges.

use_cases.title: Personal habit building
use_cases.description: Individuals use Habitica's Habits and Dailies to build consistency around exercise, sleep, study or other routines using game-based rewards.
use_cases.audience: Individuals working on personal productivity and habit formation

use_cases.title: Gamified task management for students and neurodivergent users
use_cases.description: Students and users with ADHD use Habitica's immediate feedback loops and visual progression to stay engaged with coursework and daily responsibilities.
use_cases.audience: Students and neurodivergent individuals who benefit from externalized structure

use_cases.title: Group accountability
use_cases.description: Friend groups and online communities use Parties and Guild Challenges to hold each other accountable to shared goals.
use_cases.audience: Friend groups, support communities and hobby groups

features: Full Habits, Dailies and To-Dos task system
Parties and Guilds for social accountability
Cross-device sync across web, iOS and Android
Higher monthly Gem purchase cap, up to 50 Gems
Ability to buy Gems with in-game gold

enrichment.short_description: Halaxy is a free, all-in-one practice management platform for health practitioners, covering scheduling, patient records, billing, Medicare rebates and telehealth.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium (free core platform with paid credits and subscription add-ons)
enrichment.seo_title: Halaxy Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Halaxy is free practice management software for health practitioners. See our 2026 review of features, credit-based pricing, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Halaxy is a cloud-based practice management platform built specifically for health practitioners, covering appointment scheduling, clinical documentation, billing, insurance and government rebate processing, and telehealth in a single system. It markets itself as free core software supporting more than 90 healthcare professions, from general practitioners and psychologists to physiotherapists and allied health providers, with no limit on the number of practitioners or practice locations that can use the free tier.

Halaxy's roots trace back to 2008, when co-founders Alison Hardacre, with a background in healthcare management, and Lachlan Wheeler, with an IT background, began building a practice management tool that launched online in 2012 under the name Healthkit. After raising roughly 4 million Australian dollars in funding around 2016 to 2017, the company rebuilt and repositioned its platform, relaunching it in 2019 under the name Halaxy, a portmanteau evoking a health galaxy. The company is headquartered in Melbourne, Australia, and has expanded with offices supporting Ireland, the UK and Canada as it extended the platform beyond its original Australian market into other regions with comparable healthcare billing complexity.

Halaxy runs as a web application accessible from desktop, tablet and mobile browsers, complemented by companion iOS and Android apps for practitioners and patients, so bookings, records and payments stay synced across devices. Its billing engine is built to integrate directly with government and insurance payment rails relevant to each market it serves, most notably Australia's Medicare, ECLIPSE, NDIS, DVA, Workcover and TAC schemes, automating claims that would otherwise require manual portal submissions. A built-in telehealth module handles video and phone consultations directly inside the platform, while also allowing practices to connect third-party tools like Zoom or Coviu if preferred.

The free core platform includes calendar and appointment scheduling with automated email and SMS reminders, patient record and clinical note management, more than 700 built-in clinical tools and templates covering dozens of professions, invoicing, and online booking pages patients can use directly. Halaxy's standout feature is automatic rebate processing: practices can configure Medicare and other rebates to submit automatically at a set point during or after an appointment, with responses and fund transfers happening without manual intervention, a capability the company markets as unique among practice management platforms. Telehealth, screen sharing, two-way calendar sync, a public API, white label branding and a dedicated practice phone number are available as paid subscription add-ons, while individual actions like SMS reminders, rebate processing and AI-assisted clinical scribing consume purchasable credits.

Halaxy's pricing model departs from typical SaaS subscription tiers: the core software has no license fee, no sign-up cost and no credit card requirement at signup. Instead, practices pay only for optional usage, split into two mechanisms. Pay-as-you-go credits, used for actions like SMS reminders, rebate submissions, telehealth minutes and AI Scribe transcription, cost around 0.15 to 0.22 Australian dollars each depending on volume, with bulk purchases reducing the per-credit price. Subscription Features, a separate monthly add-on starting around 25 Australian dollars per month, unlock structural capabilities like two-way calendar sync, white label branding, API access, e-script integrations and a dedicated phone number that aren't available on pay-as-you-go credits alone.

Halaxy is built for solo practitioners and small-to-midsize allied health and medical clinics, particularly in Australia where its rebate automation for Medicare, NDIS, DVA and workers' compensation schemes delivers the clearest advantage, though it has extended support into the UK and Ireland markets. Typical users include psychologists, physiotherapists, GPs, dietitians, podiatrists, occupational therapists, and other allied health professionals who need integrated scheduling, clinical notes and compliant billing without maintaining multiple disconnected systems.

Within Australia's practice management category, Halaxy competes with platforms like Best Practice, Medical Director, Cliniko and Power Diary. Its key differentiator is the free-core, pay-per-use pricing model, which undercuts competitors that charge flat monthly subscription fees regardless of usage, making it attractive to smaller or lower-volume practices. Its deep, automated integration with Australian government rebate schemes is a particular strength versus more generic international scheduling tools. The tradeoff is that heavy users of SMS reminders, telehealth or rebate processing can end up paying meaningfully through credits, and some of the platform's most valuable structural features, like the API and two-way sync, sit behind the separate paid subscription tier rather than being included for free.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Halaxy is a free, cloud-based practice management platform for health practitioners, combining scheduling, clinical documentation, billing and telehealth in one system. It supports more than 90 healthcare professions and places no limit on practitioners or locations on its free tier.

Founded in Melbourne, Australia, and launched online in 2012 (originally as Healthkit before rebranding to Halaxy in 2019), the company has since expanded support into the UK and Ireland markets alongside its core Australian base.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Halaxy's free core includes calendar scheduling with automated reminders, clinical notes, over 700 clinical templates, invoicing and online patient booking. Its standout capability is automated processing of Medicare, NDIS, DVA, Workcover and TAC rebates without manual portal submission.

Built-in telehealth supports video and phone consultations directly inside the platform, and paid add-ons unlock two-way calendar sync, white label branding, API access and e-script integrations for practices that need deeper customization.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Halaxy's core software is free with no sign-up cost or credit card requirement. Usage-based Pay-As-You-Go credits, roughly A$0.15 to A$0.22 each, cover actions like SMS reminders, rebate processing and telehealth minutes.
Subscription Features, starting around A$25 per month, unlock structural capabilities like the API, two-way sync and white label branding that aren't available through credits alone.

Automates Medicare, NDIS and DVA rebate processing without manual work

Some advanced features depend on purchasing credits in bulk to get better rates

faqs.question: Is Halaxy really free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Halaxy's core practice management platform, including scheduling, patient records, clinical notes and invoicing, is free with no sign-up cost or credit card required. Practices only pay for optional credits and subscription add-ons.

faqs.question: What is Halaxy used for?
faqs.answer: Halaxy is used by health practitioners to manage appointment scheduling, clinical documentation, billing, insurance and government rebates, and telehealth consultations in one platform.

faqs.question: Does Halaxy process Medicare rebates automatically?
faqs.answer: Yes, Halaxy can be configured to automatically submit Medicare and other rebates at a set time during or after an appointment, with responses and fund transfers happening without manual intervention.

faqs.question: Does Halaxy support telehealth?
faqs.answer: Yes, Halaxy includes a built-in telehealth module for video and phone consultations, and can also connect to external platforms like Zoom or Coviu.

faqs.question: What are Halaxy credits used for?
faqs.answer: Halaxy credits are a pay-as-you-go currency used for actions like SMS reminders, rebate processing, telehealth minutes and AI Scribe transcription.

faqs.question: Which countries does Halaxy support?
faqs.answer: Halaxy was built primarily for the Australian healthcare system and its rebate schemes, and has since expanded to support practices in the UK and Ireland.

use_cases.title: Solo allied health practitioner
use_cases.description: A single practitioner uses Halaxy's free core platform to manage bookings, clinical notes and invoicing without paying for software.
use_cases.audience: Solo practitioners such as psychologists, physiotherapists or dietitians

use_cases.title: Multi-practitioner clinic billing automation
use_cases.description: Clinics use Halaxy's automated Medicare, NDIS and DVA rebate processing to reduce administrative billing work across multiple practitioners.
use_cases.audience: Multi-practitioner clinics handling government and insurance billing

use_cases.title: Telehealth-first practice
use_cases.description: Practices delivering remote care use Halaxy's built-in telehealth alongside integrated scheduling and rebate processing.
use_cases.audience: Practitioners offering remote or hybrid telehealth consultations

pricing_plans.price: Approximately A$0.15 to A$0.22 per credit

enrichment.short_description: HaloITSM is an all-inclusive, ITIL-aligned IT service management platform covering incidents, requests, changes, problems and assets with a built-in AI copilot.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom pricing, typically around $50 per agent per month
enrichment.seo_title: HaloITSM Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: HaloITSM is an all-inclusive ITSM platform for incidents, requests, changes and assets. Read our 2026 review of features, pricing, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
HaloITSM is a unified IT service management platform built around the ITIL framework, consolidating incident management, service requests, change management, problem management, a configuration management database, asset tracking and knowledge management into a single all-inclusive product rather than selling each capability as a separate module.

The product is developed by Halo Service Solutions, a UK software company whose roots go back to 1994, when it was founded as NetHelpDesk, an early help desk software vendor. The company operated under the NetHelpDesk name for roughly two and a half decades before undertaking a full browser-based rewrite of its product line, relaunching in 2019 under the Halo brand. Since that rebrand, Halo Service Solutions has expanded its single ITSM product into a family that includes HaloITSM for enterprise IT service management, HaloPSA for managed service providers, and HaloCRM for customer service teams, all built on a shared underlying platform. The company is headquartered in Stowmarket, Suffolk, in the United Kingdom, employs more than 250 people, and reports serving over 100,000 users across more than 75 countries, including large enterprise and public sector customers such as Microsoft and FTSE 100 companies.

HaloITSM is offered as a cloud-hosted SaaS product but also supports private cloud and fully on-premise deployment, which distinguishes it from many SaaS-only ITSM competitors and appeals to organizations with strict data residency or compliance requirements. The platform includes a browser-based agent console and a self-service portal for end users, a configurable workflow engine that underlies incident, request, change and problem processes, and Halo AI, a built-in artificial intelligence layer used for ticket triage, categorization, suggested responses and automation.

Core capabilities include incident and major incident management with SLA tracking and escalation, a configurable service catalog with self-service request forms and automated approval routing, change management supporting standard, normal and emergency change types with Change Advisory Board review workflows, problem management with root cause analysis and known error documentation, a CMDB for tracking assets and their service dependencies, and a reporting suite offering more than 1,000 out-of-the-box reports alongside custom dashboard building. Halo AI adds automated ticket categorization, prioritization and drafting assistance on top of these core ITIL processes.

HaloITSM does not publish fixed pricing tiers on its website; instead it uses per-agent, per-month pricing where the final quote depends on team size, deployment method, cloud, private cloud or on-premise, and contract length. Third-party analyses put typical costs in the range of roughly 50 to 70 US dollars per agent per month with all ITIL modules, integrations and asset management included, with per-agent pricing declining at scale, reportedly by around 25 percent for organizations with more than 100 agents. Small teams of five to ten agents have been estimated to pay roughly 3,000 to 8,400 US dollars per year, mid-market teams of 20 to 50 agents roughly 12,000 to 42,000 US dollars per year, and enterprise deployments of 100 or more agents from roughly 60,000 US dollars per year upward, though these figures come from third-party pricing guides rather than Halo's own published rate card. The company has also promoted a limited-time all-inclusive enterprise offering for qualifying organizations, and it provides a 30-day free trial with full feature access rather than a limited freemium tier.

HaloITSM targets mid-market to enterprise IT departments, managed service providers, and organizations in regulated sectors such as healthcare, financial services, government and education that need a compliant, ITIL-aligned service desk with flexible deployment options. Its all-inclusive pricing model, which bundles ITSM modules together instead of charging separately per feature, appeals particularly to organizations that want predictable per-agent costs without negotiating add-on fees as they adopt more ITIL processes.

HaloITSM competes against established ITSM platforms such as ServiceNow, Freshservice, Jira Service Management and Ivanti Neurons, as well as against its own sibling product HaloPSA, which targets MSPs specifically. Its main differentiators are an all-inclusive pricing structure that avoids the module-by-module upsells common among larger competitors like ServiceNow, flexible on-premise and private cloud deployment options that many SaaS-only rivals lack, and a three-decade operating history under Halo Service Solutions and its NetHelpDesk predecessor that gives it deep ITIL domain experience. The tradeoff versus lighter-weight competitors like Freshservice is that HaloITSM's breadth of ITIL functionality can mean a steeper configuration and onboarding curve, and because pricing isn't published, prospective buyers must go through a sales quote process to get exact costs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: HaloITSM is an all-inclusive, ITIL-aligned IT service management platform that bundles incident, request, change, problem, asset and knowledge management into one product rather than separately priced modules.

It is developed by Halo Service Solutions, a UK company founded in 1994 as NetHelpDesk that rebranded to Halo in 2019 after a full browser-based product rewrite, and is headquartered in Stowmarket, England, with over 250 employees.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core capabilities span incident and major incident management with SLA tracking, a self-service catalog with approval routing, change management with CAB review workflows, problem management with root cause analysis, and a CMDB for asset and dependency tracking.

Halo AI adds automated ticket triage, categorization and drafting assistance, while the platform offers over 1,000 built-in reports and flexible deployment across cloud, private cloud or fully on-premise environments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: HaloITSM uses custom, per-agent monthly pricing rather than published tiers, with quotes depending on team size, deployment method and contract length; third-party estimates put typical costs around $50 to $70 per agent per month with all modules included.
Volume discounts apply at scale, and the company offers a 30-day free trial with full feature access along with a promoted all-inclusive enterprise package for qualifying organizations.

faqs.answer: HaloITSM uses custom per-agent monthly pricing based on team size, deployment method and contract length; third-party estimates put typical costs around $50 to $70 per agent per month with all modules included.

faqs.answer: Yes, HaloITSM offers a 30-day free trial with full access to its features.

faqs.answer: Halo AI is HaloITSM's built-in artificial intelligence layer that automates ticket categorization, prioritization and response drafting.

faqs.answer: Yes, HaloITSM supports cloud, private cloud and fully on-premise deployment options.

enrichment.short_description: HandBrake is a free, open source video transcoder that converts video from nearly any format into modern codecs like H.264, H.265, AV1 and VP9.
enrichment.seo_title: HandBrake Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: HandBrake is a free open source video converter for Windows, Mac and Linux. Read our 2026 review of features, formats supported, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
HandBrake is a free, open source video transcoder that converts video files and DVD or Blu-ray sources from almost any input format into a curated set of modern, widely supported output codecs and containers, most commonly H.264, H.265 (HEVC), AV1, VP8 and VP9 wrapped in MP4 or MKV containers. It is distributed under the GNU General Public License version 2 and runs natively on Windows, macOS and Linux, making it one of the most widely used free video conversion tools across all three major desktop platforms.

HandBrake was originally created in 2003 by developer Eric Petit as a tool for ripping DVDs on BeOS, before being ported to other platforms. Petit remained the primary developer until around 2006, when active development on his codebase stalled. Later that year, two developers, Rodney Hester and Chris Long, who had independently been reverse-engineering the H.264 encoder embedded in Apple's iPod firmware, met on the HandBrake forums and combined their work into an unofficial fork they called MediaFork. In February 2007, Petit reached out to endorse their continued development, and the project was eventually consolidated back under the HandBrake name with H.264 support integrated. After more than a decade of incremental community-driven development, the project reached its first stable 1.0.0 release on December 24, 2016, and has continued shipping regular updates since, maintained entirely by volunteer contributors rather than a company.

HandBrake's own original codebase is relatively small; the application is primarily an integration layer that wires together a collection of well-established third-party audio and video libraries, including FFmpeg for demuxing and decoding, and dedicated encoders like x264 and x265 for H.264 and H.265 output, alongside components for deinterlacing, denoising, scaling and subtitle handling. This architecture lets HandBrake track improvements in the underlying codec libraries without having to reimplement encoding logic itself, and it exposes both a graphical desktop application and a separate command-line interface, HandBrakeCLI, for scripting and batch automation.

Core capabilities include broad source-format compatibility, accepting nearly any common video container or file as input, device- and platform-specific encoding presets that simplify choosing appropriate settings for phones, tablets, web upload or archival quality, a batch queue for converting multiple files unattended, and a filter chain covering deinterlacing, denoising, rotation, cropping and scaling. It supports chapter markers, subtitle passthrough and burn-in, multiple audio track handling, and hardware-accelerated encoding paths such as Apple VideoToolbox, Intel Quick Sync Video and NVIDIA NVENC where available, which significantly speeds up conversion on supported hardware. The preset system in particular has made HandBrake a common recommendation for non-technical users who want good default output settings without manually tuning encoder parameters.

HandBrake is entirely free to download and use, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, watermarks or feature restrictions of any kind. As a volunteer-maintained open source project, it accepts donations to support ongoing development but does not sell a commercial version; the source code is publicly available on GitHub for anyone to inspect, modify or build from source, which has helped sustain community trust in the tool over more than two decades of continuous development.

HandBrake's users span home users converting personal DVD collections or downloaded video into space-efficient formats for storage or playback on phones and tablets, content creators and video editors who need to compress or reformat footage for web delivery, and IT professionals or developers who use the command-line HandBrakeCLI to automate video conversion pipelines at scale. Its zero cost and cross-platform availability make it a default recommendation across enthusiast and professional communities alike, and its long operating history has made it a trusted, well-understood tool rather than a niche or unproven utility.

HandBrake is frequently compared to other free tools like FFmpeg, which HandBrake itself builds on but which requires command-line expertise, and to paid consumer transcoding software such as Adobe Media Encoder or commercial DVD-ripping utilities. Its main differentiator is combining a genuinely free, ad-free, open source license with an accessible graphical interface and sensible presets, striking a middle ground between raw FFmpeg, powerful but unfriendly for non-technical users, and paid GUI tools that charge for equivalent functionality. Because it deliberately excludes DVD and Blu-ray decryption logic to remain legally distributable, it typically requires a separate library such as libdvdcss for encrypted disc sources, which is the most common limitation cited by reviewers relative to some commercial ripping tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: HandBrake is a free, open source video transcoder that converts video from nearly any format into modern codecs such as H.264, H.265, AV1 and VP9, distributed under the GPLv2 license and available on Windows, macOS and Linux.

Originally created in 2003 by Eric Petit for BeOS, HandBrake was later revived and extended by community developers who added H.264 support, reaching its first stable 1.0.0 release in December 2016 after more than a decade of volunteer-driven development.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: HandBrake accepts nearly any video source as input and offers device-specific presets, a batch queue for converting multiple files, and a filter chain for deinterlacing, denoising, cropping and scaling.

Hardware-accelerated encoding via Apple VideoToolbox, Intel Quick Sync and NVIDIA NVENC speeds up conversion, while a separate command-line interface, HandBrakeCLI, supports scripting and automated batch workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: HandBrake is completely free to download and use, with no paid tiers, subscriptions, watermarks or feature restrictions.
As a volunteer-maintained open source project, it relies on donations rather than sales, and its source code is publicly available on GitHub for anyone to inspect or modify.

faqs.question: Is HandBrake free?
faqs.answer: Yes, HandBrake is completely free and open source, with no paid tiers, subscriptions or feature restrictions.

faqs.question: What video formats does HandBrake support?
faqs.answer: HandBrake accepts nearly any common video source as input and can output to modern codecs including H.264, H.265, AV1, VP8 and VP9, typically wrapped in MP4 or MKV containers.

faqs.question: Does HandBrake work on Mac and Linux?
faqs.answer: Yes, HandBrake runs natively on Windows, macOS and Linux.

faqs.question: Can HandBrake rip encrypted DVDs?
faqs.answer: HandBrake does not include DVD or Blu-ray decryption logic by default to remain legally distributable, so encrypted discs typically require a separate library such as libdvdcss.

faqs.question: Is there a command-line version of HandBrake?
faqs.answer: Yes, HandBrakeCLI is a separate command-line interface that supports scripting and automated batch video conversion.

faqs.question: Who maintains HandBrake?
faqs.answer: HandBrake is maintained by volunteer open source contributors rather than a company, with development ongoing on GitHub since the project's creation in 2003.

enrichment.short_description: Hanko is an open source, developer-first authentication platform focused on passkeys, offering passwordless and social login with a generous free tier.
enrichment.seo_title: Hanko Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Hanko is an open source passkey authentication platform. Read our 2026 review of features, MAU-based pricing, pros, cons and alternatives to Auth0.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Hanko is a developer-focused authentication and user-management platform built around passkeys, the FIDO2/WebAuthn-based passwordless login standard, alongside supporting traditional passwords, passwordless email or magic-link login, and social single sign-on. It ships as both a hosted cloud service and an open source, self-hostable core, positioning itself as an open alternative to proprietary identity providers like Auth0, Clerk, WorkOS and Stytch.

Hanko was founded in Kiel, Germany, in 2018 by Felix Magedanz, building on ideas the founding team had explored as early as 2015 around the belief that passkeys would eventually replace passwords for online authentication. The company initially launched as a closed-source, enterprise-focused authentication vendor, landing early customers including SAP, but found the market too early since general awareness of passkeys was minimal at the time. Hanko pivoted its business model and made its core authentication server open source in 2022, aligning the product with the growing industry-wide push toward FIDO2 passkeys led by the FIDO Alliance and major platform vendors. In May 2023, Hanko raised a venture funding round of approximately 1.2 million euros led by adesso ventures, with participation from High-Tech Grunderfonds, Smart Infrastructure Ventures and several angel investors, to fund continued development of its passkey infrastructure.

Hanko's core authentication server is open source and can be self-hosted, giving developers full control over their authentication data, while Hanko Cloud offers the same functionality as a managed service so teams can avoid running their own infrastructure. The platform provides a lightweight HTTP API and client SDKs, built on years of the founding team's FIDO2 and WebAuthn implementation experience, so developers can add passkey support to an existing application's login flow without building WebAuthn ceremonies from scratch, alongside prebuilt UI components for faster integration.

Hanko supports passkey registration and login as its primary differentiator, alongside fallback options including traditional passwords, passwordless email login, and social SSO providers such as Google and Apple. It includes multi-factor authentication, an admin dashboard and Admin API for managing users and projects, webhook support for reacting to authentication events, custom domain support so login flows stay on a company's own domain, and custom SMTP configuration for transactional email delivery. Higher tiers add team member invites for managing access collaboratively and optional SAML SSO for enterprise identity federation.

Hanko's Starter plan is free and includes up to 10,000 monthly active users, defined as any user issued a session token within a given month, across up to 2 projects, with passkeys, passwords, passwordless login, social SSO, multi-factor authentication, a custom domain, an admin dashboard and community support, and no credit card is required to start. The Pro plan costs 29 US dollars per month plus 0.01 dollars per MAU beyond the included 10,000, supports up to 5 projects, and adds personal support via email and Slack, team member invites, webhooks, the Admin API, custom SMTP, and optional SAML SSO priced at an additional 49 dollars per month per connection. An Enterprise plan offers custom, volume-discounted pricing with unlimited projects, a 99.99 percent uptime SLA, priority support, admin dashboard SSO, extended audit log retention, a commercial license option and private cloud deployment. Hanko also runs a Startup Plan that grants up to one million free monthly active users to qualifying early-stage companies that apply.

Hanko targets software engineering teams, particularly at startups and mid-size SaaS companies, who want to add modern passkey-first login without building and maintaining WebAuthn infrastructure themselves, as well as security-conscious organizations that want to reduce phishing and credential-stuffing risk by moving users away from passwords. Its open source self-hosting option also appeals to developers who need full data control for compliance reasons rather than relying solely on a third-party-hosted identity provider.

In the customer identity and access management space, Hanko competes with both established players like Auth0 and Okta and newer developer-first entrants like Clerk, WorkOS, SuperTokens and Stytch. Its core differentiation is being open source at its foundation while still offering a fully managed cloud option, combined with a passkey-first product philosophy rather than treating passkeys as one option among many bolted onto a legacy password-based system. The generous free tier of 10,000 MAUs, and the startup program's offer of up to one million free MAUs, make it particularly attractive to early-stage companies price-sensitive about per-user authentication costs, though its smaller company size and shorter open source track record since 2022 mean it has a less mature ecosystem and community than larger incumbents.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Hanko is an open source, developer-first authentication platform built around passkeys, offering passwordless login, traditional passwords, social SSO and multi-factor authentication through a hosted cloud service or self-hosted core.
Founded in Kiel, Germany, in 2018 by Felix Magedanz, Hanko began as a closed-source enterprise vendor before making its authentication server open source in 2022 and raising a venture round in 2023.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Hanko's primary differentiator is native passkey (FIDO2/WebAuthn) support alongside fallback password, passwordless email and social SSO login options, delivered via a lightweight HTTP API and client SDKs.
The platform also includes multi-factor authentication, an admin dashboard and Admin API, webhooks, custom domains, custom SMTP, and optional SAML SSO for enterprise identity federation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Hanko's free Starter plan includes up to 10,000 monthly active users across 2 projects with no credit card required, while the Pro plan costs $29/month plus $0.01 per MAU beyond 10,000.
Enterprise pricing is custom with an uptime SLA and private cloud deployment, and a Startup Plan grants up to 1 million free MAUs to qualifying early-stage companies.

faqs.question: What is Hanko?
faqs.answer: Hanko is an open source authentication platform focused on passkeys, offering passwordless login, passwords, social SSO and multi-factor authentication via a hosted cloud service or self-hosted core.

faqs.question: Is Hanko open source?
faqs.answer: Yes, Hanko's core authentication server became open source in 2022 and can be self-hosted, while Hanko Cloud offers the same functionality as a managed service.

faqs.question: How much does Hanko cost?
faqs.answer: Hanko's Starter plan is free for up to 10,000 monthly active users. The Pro plan costs $29/month plus $0.01 per MAU beyond 10,000, and Enterprise pricing is custom.

faqs.question: What are passkeys and why does Hanko use them?
faqs.answer: Passkeys are a FIDO2/WebAuthn-based passwordless login standard that replace passwords with device-based cryptographic credentials, reducing phishing and credential-stuffing risk, which is why Hanko built its platform around them.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Hanko?
faqs.answer: Yes, Hanko's core authentication server is open source and can be self-hosted for teams that need full control over their authentication data.

faqs.question: What counts as a monthly active user in Hanko's pricing?
faqs.answer: Hanko defines a monthly active user as any user who is issued a session token at least once during a given month.

enrichment.short_description: HappyFox is an AI-powered, omnichannel help desk platform for customer service, IT and HR teams, unifying ticketing across email, chat, phone and social.
enrichment.seo_title: HappyFox Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: HappyFox is an AI-powered omnichannel help desk for support, IT and HR teams. Read our 2026 review of features, pricing tiers, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
HappyFox is a customer support and help desk software suite that unifies ticketing across email, live chat, phone and social media channels for customer service, IT support and human resources teams. Its product lineup includes a core Help Desk, a Service Desk built for ITIL-style IT support, Live Chat, a chatbot, workflow automation, and business intelligence reporting, all designed to plug into a shared ticketing backbone.

HappyFox was founded in 2011 by Shalin Jain and has remained a self-funded, bootstrapped company throughout its history, without taking outside venture capital, a notable contrast to many SaaS competitors that raised large funding rounds. The company is headquartered in Irvine, California, and has grown steadily on the back of that bootstrapped model, reporting annual revenue of roughly 20 million US dollars in 2024, up from about 13.7 million US dollars in 2023, with a team of roughly 120 employees. HappyFox Help Desk has been recognized by PC Magazine as a top-rated help desk product for multiple consecutive years, a distinction the company highlights heavily in its marketing.

HappyFox is delivered as a cloud-based SaaS platform accessible through a web dashboard, with dedicated mobile apps for iOS and Android that let agents triage, respond to and track tickets from a phone or tablet, keeping mobile and desktop views in sync. The platform is built around a unified ticketing data model that lets separate teams, customer support, IT, HR, and other internal functions, run on the same underlying system while keeping their queues, workflows and permissions separate, and it connects to more than 100 third-party tools including Slack, Salesforce, Jira and Microsoft products via native integrations.

Core capabilities include AI-powered ticketing with an AI Copilot suite that provides instant answer suggestions, resolution recommendations, response drafting assistance and automatic urgency prioritization, an omnichannel inbox that consolidates email, chat, phone and social interactions into one queue, and a self-service knowledge base portal that lets end users resolve common issues without opening a ticket. The platform also includes workflow automation to route and escalate tickets without manual intervention, real-time analytics and reporting dashboards, and enterprise-grade security features including granular role-based access controls. Because Help Desk, Service Desk, Live Chat, workflow automation and BI reporting are built as natively integrated but separately licensed products, teams can adopt only the modules relevant to them.

HappyFox Help Desk uses per-agent monthly pricing with three published tiers: Basic at 24 US dollars per agent per month capped at 5 agents, Team at 49 dollars per agent per month, and Pro at 99 dollars per agent per month, all with unlimited agents above the Basic tier, plus a custom-priced Enterprise Pro tier for large organizations; annual billing saves roughly 20 percent versus monthly billing. For larger or high-ticket-volume teams, HappyFox also offers Unlimited Agent plans that charge a flat annual fee with a fixed yearly ticket allowance instead of per-agent pricing, which the company positions as more economical and predictable once a team exceeds roughly 20 to 25 agents. Adjacent products like Live Chat, the AI features, Workflows and BI reporting are priced and purchased separately from Help Desk, so customers only pay for the modules they actually use. Every plan includes a 14-day free trial with no credit card required.

HappyFox serves customer service, IT support, HR and operations teams across company sizes from small businesses to large enterprises, with particular traction in education, healthcare, ecommerce, real estate, manufacturing and government sectors that need a help desk flexible enough to serve both external customers and internal employees from one platform.

HappyFox competes against Zendesk, Freshdesk, Zoho Desk and Freshservice in the help desk and ITSM markets. Its key differentiators are a genuinely bootstrapped, profitable business model that has let it avoid the pricing pressure that comes with venture-backed growth targets, an unlimited-agent flat-fee pricing option that larger support teams can use to avoid unpredictable per-seat cost growth, and a single platform that natively spans customer support, IT and HR ticketing rather than requiring separate tools per department. Its AI Copilot suite brings it into direct feature parity with AI-forward competitors like Zendesk's and Intercom's AI tooling, while its per-agent entry pricing on the Basic tier remains competitive for small teams evaluating help desk software for the first time.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: HappyFox is a cloud-based help desk and customer support suite that unifies email, chat, phone and social ticketing for customer service, IT and HR teams, delivered through a shared ticketing backbone with separately licensed add-on modules.

Founded in 2011 by Shalin Jain and headquartered in Irvine, California, HappyFox has remained bootstrapped without outside venture funding, reporting roughly $20 million in revenue in 2024 with about 120 employees.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: An AI Copilot suite provides instant answer suggestions, resolution recommendations and automatic urgency prioritization, layered on top of an omnichannel inbox that consolidates email, chat, phone and social interactions.

A self-service knowledge base, workflow automation, real-time analytics and role-based access controls round out the platform, which integrates with over 100 tools including Slack, Salesforce and Jira.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: HappyFox Help Desk starts at $24 per agent per month on the Basic plan (capped at 5 agents), scaling to Team at $49 and Pro at $99 per agent per month with unlimited agents, plus custom Enterprise Pro pricing.
Unlimited Agent plans charge a flat annual fee with a fixed ticket allowance for larger teams, and every plan includes a 14-day free trial with no credit card required.

faqs.question: What is HappyFox used for?

faqs.question: How much does HappyFox cost?
faqs.answer: HappyFox Help Desk starts at $24 per agent per month on the Basic plan, with Team at $49 and Pro at $99 per agent per month, plus a custom-priced Enterprise Pro tier and flat-fee Unlimited Agent plans for larger teams.

faqs.question: Does HappyFox offer a free trial?
faqs.answer: Yes, every HappyFox plan includes a 14-day free trial with no credit card required.

faqs.question: What is HappyFox AI Copilot?
faqs.answer: AI Copilot is HappyFox's AI-powered suite that provides instant answer suggestions, resolution recommendations, response drafting assistance and automatic urgency prioritization for support agents.

faqs.question: Does HappyFox support IT and HR use cases beyond customer support?
faqs.answer: Yes, HappyFox is used by IT and HR teams as well as customer support, with a Service Desk product built for ITIL-style IT support alongside the core Help Desk.

faqs.question: Are there unlimited-agent pricing options?
faqs.answer: Yes, HappyFox offers Unlimited Agent plans that charge a flat annual fee with a fixed yearly ticket allowance, which tends to be more economical for teams above roughly 20 to 25 agents.

enrichment.short_description: Haraka is a free, open source, high-performance SMTP mail server built in Node.js with a fully extensible JavaScript plugin architecture.
enrichment.seo_title: Haraka Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Haraka is a free open source Node.js SMTP server with a plugin architecture. Read our 2026 review of features, use cases, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Haraka is a free, open source SMTP mail server written in Node.js, designed to handle extremely high volumes of concurrent email connections through an asynchronous, event-driven architecture, while remaining fully customizable through a JavaScript plugin system. It is used both as an inbound filtering mail gateway and as an outbound mail submission agent, and it is released under the permissive MIT license.

Haraka was created by Matt Sergeant, previously a project leader of SpamAssassin and a contributor to the Qpsmtpd mail server, drawing on his experience building high-performance spam-trap infrastructure at MessageLabs, an email security company later acquired by Symantec, where handling thousands of concurrent, high-latency SMTP connections efficiently was a core operational requirement. Early versions of Haraka were released starting in 2011, explicitly inspired by Qpsmtpd's plugin-based design but reimagined for Node.js's asynchronous I/O model. Haraka 2.0, released in November 2012, was a major overhaul that adopted Node.js's Stream API internals for better throughput, and Haraka 3.0, released in December 2022, modernized the codebase with contemporary JavaScript patterns like optional chaining and Promise-based async handling. The project has no corporate owner; it is community-maintained on GitHub, currently led by maintainer Matt Simerson.

Haraka's architecture centers on Node.js's non-blocking, event-driven I/O model, which lets a single server process handle thousands of simultaneous SMTP connections without the thread- or process-per-connection overhead that limits some traditional MTAs. Behavior is customized entirely through a plugin system, where JavaScript plugins hook into defined stages of the SMTP transaction, connection, HELO, MAIL FROM, RCPT TO, DATA, and queueing, to inspect, modify, accept or reject messages, giving developers full programmatic control without patching server internals.

Haraka ships with a large registry of core and community plugins covering authentication, DKIM signing and verification, DNS blocklist (DNSBL) checking, HELO validation, integration with SpamAssassin and rspamd for spam scoring, ClamAV for antivirus scanning, Redis-backed caching, and multiple queueing and delivery backends. It supports being deployed either as a filtering inbound mail transfer agent that hands validated mail to a downstream MTA like Postfix, or as a full mail submission agent handling authenticated outbound mail on the standard submission ports with DKIM signing enabled.

Haraka is completely free and open source under the MIT license, with no paid tiers, commercial edition or vendor lock-in; anyone can download, modify, self-host and redistribute it without cost, and there is no company selling a hosted or supported commercial version.

Haraka is used by engineering teams that need to operate their own high-throughput mail infrastructure rather than relying entirely on third-party email service providers, including companies filtering large volumes of inbound mail and platforms building custom transactional email pipelines. Notable production users include Craigslist, which has used Haraka to handle inbound mail filtering and validation before forwarding accepted connections to Postfix, reportedly reducing hardware requirements after adopting it, and email infrastructure companies like Bounce.io, which has used Haraka to accept and process thousands of simultaneous SMTP connections.

Within the mail transfer agent category, Haraka sits alongside long-established MTAs like Postfix, Exim and Sendmail, as well as other plugin-driven servers like Qpsmtpd, which directly inspired it. Its main differentiator is being built in JavaScript and Node.js rather than C or Perl, which makes it dramatically easier for teams already working in a JavaScript-heavy stack to write custom plugins and extend mail-handling logic without learning a new language or a rigid configuration-file syntax. This comes at the cost of a smaller install base and community than Postfix or Exim, and Haraka is squarely aimed at engineering teams comfortable operating and extending their own mail server rather than organizations that want a fully managed, GUI-driven email platform. For teams that need high-throughput, programmable mail handling and already have Node.js expertise in-house, Haraka remains one of the few open source MTAs built specifically around that workflow.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Haraka is a free, open source SMTP mail server written in Node.js, built for high-throughput, asynchronous mail handling with a fully extensible JavaScript plugin architecture, released under the MIT license.
Created by Matt Sergeant in 2011 and inspired by the Qpsmtpd mail server, Haraka is community-maintained on GitHub with no corporate owner, currently led by maintainer Matt Simerson.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Haraka's event-driven, non-blocking architecture lets a single server process handle thousands of simultaneous SMTP connections, with behavior customized entirely through JavaScript plugins hooked into each stage of the SMTP transaction.

A large plugin registry covers authentication, DKIM signing, DNS blocklist checking, SpamAssassin and rspamd integration, and ClamAV antivirus scanning, and Haraka can run as either an inbound filtering gateway or an outbound submission agent.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Haraka is completely free and open source under the MIT license, with no paid tiers, commercial edition or vendor lock-in.
Anyone can download, modify, self-host and redistribute Haraka without cost, and there is no company selling a hosted or supported commercial version.

features.title: Event-driven architecture
features.description: Node.js's non-blocking I/O model lets a single process handle thousands of simultaneous SMTP connections.

features.title: JavaScript plugin system
features.description: Plugins hook into every stage of the SMTP transaction to inspect, modify, accept or reject messages.

features.title: DKIM signing and verification
features.description: Built-in support for signing and verifying DKIM to authenticate outbound and inbound mail.

features.title: DNS blocklist checking
features.description: Plugins check DNSBLs to filter known spam and abuse sources before accepting mail.

features.title: Spam and antivirus integration
features.description: Integrates with SpamAssassin, rspamd and ClamAV for spam scoring and malware scanning.

features.title: Flexible deployment modes
features.description: Runs as an inbound filtering gateway in front of Postfix or as a full outbound mail submission agent.

features.title: High connection throughput
features.description: Handles thousands of concurrent connections and messages per second in production.

features.title: MIT licensed and free
features.description: Fully open source with no cost, commercial edition or licensing restrictions.

pros: Free and open source under the permissive MIT license
Handles very high SMTP connection volume and throughput
Highly extensible through a JavaScript plugin architecture
Long track record in production since 2011
Battle-tested at scale by companies like Craigslist
cons: Requires Node.js and JavaScript expertise to configure and extend
No official graphical management dashboard
Documentation is community-maintained and can be sparse in places
Not a hosted or managed SaaS, so self-hosting carries operational burden
Smaller community and install base than Postfix or Exim

faqs.question: What is Haraka?
faqs.answer: Haraka is a free, open source SMTP mail server written in Node.js, designed for high-throughput, asynchronous mail handling with a fully extensible JavaScript plugin architecture.

faqs.question: Is Haraka free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Haraka is completely free and open source under the MIT license, with no paid tiers or commercial edition.

faqs.question: Who maintains Haraka?
faqs.answer: Haraka was created by Matt Sergeant and is now community-maintained on GitHub, currently led by maintainer Matt Simerson.

faqs.question: What is Haraka used for?
faqs.answer: Haraka is used to operate high-throughput inbound mail filtering gateways or outbound mail submission agents, and is used in production by companies like Craigslist.

faqs.question: Does Haraka support spam filtering?
faqs.answer: Yes, Haraka integrates with SpamAssassin and rspamd for spam scoring and supports DNS blocklist checking through plugins.

faqs.question: Can I write custom plugins for Haraka?
faqs.answer: Yes, Haraka's behavior is customized entirely through JavaScript plugins that hook into each stage of the SMTP transaction.

use_cases.title: High-throughput inbound mail filtering
use_cases.description: Companies use Haraka as a filtering gateway that validates and forwards inbound mail to a downstream MTA like Postfix, as Craigslist does in production.
use_cases.audience: Engineering teams operating high-volume inbound mail infrastructure

use_cases.title: Custom transactional email infrastructure
use_cases.description: Developers build custom outbound mail pipelines using Haraka's plugin system instead of relying entirely on third-party email service providers.
use_cases.audience: Developers building transactional email systems

use_cases.title: Self-hosted, extensible MTA
use_cases.description: Organizations that want full control over mail-handling logic self-host Haraka instead of using a commercial ESP.
use_cases.audience: Organizations wanting a self-hosted, programmable mail server

enrichment.short_description: Harbor is a CNCF-graduated, open source container registry that stores, scans and signs container images with role-based access control for Kubernetes.
enrichment.seo_title: Harbor Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Harbor is a CNCF-graduated open source container registry with scanning and signing. Read our 2026 review of features, deployment, pros, cons and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Harbor is an open source, cloud-native container registry that stores, secures, signs and scans container images and other OCI artifacts such as Helm charts, giving organizations a private alternative to public registries like Docker Hub with policy enforcement and role-based access control built in.

Harbor originated inside VMware's China research and development organization in 2014, built internally to give VMware a way to assess the security posture of container images and enforce compliance policies before those images were used internally. VMware open sourced the project in March 2016, making the codebase publicly available for the broader developer community to use and contribute to. Harbor joined the Cloud Native Computing Foundation as a hosted project on July 31, 2018, and graduated to CNCF Graduated status on June 15, 2020, becoming the eleventh project to reach that maturity level and the first open source container registry to do so. Since joining CNCF, Harbor has received contributions from more than 680 organizations, though VMware has continued to account for a large majority, roughly 72 percent, of cumulative contributions, reflecting its origin as a VMware-incubated project that later became a genuinely community-governed one.

Harbor is designed to deploy on any Kubernetes cluster via an official Helm chart or on a standalone system with Docker support, and it exposes both a web-based management UI and a RESTful API for automation. Its architecture integrates external vulnerability scanners, most commonly Trivy or Clair, to analyze stored images for known vulnerabilities, and supports content trust and image signing through integration with tools like Notary and Cosign so consumers can verify that an image came from a trusted source before deploying it. Harbor's multi-tenancy model organizes images into projects with granular role-based access control that can integrate with existing identity systems such as LDAP, Active Directory and OIDC providers.

Core capabilities include automated vulnerability scanning of stored images with policy-based blocking of images that fail security checks, content trust and digital signing for image provenance and integrity, project-based multi-tenancy with fine-grained role-based access control, cross-registry replication for mirroring images between multiple Harbor instances or clouds, a built-in Helm chart repository for storing Kubernetes packaging artifacts, image retention and garbage collection policies to manage storage, and both a full web UI and API-first automation support for integrating Harbor into CI/CD pipelines.

Harbor itself is entirely free and open source, released under the Apache 2.0 license, with no paid tier, license fee or usage limit imposed by the project; organizations self-host Harbor on their own infrastructure or Kubernetes clusters and bear only their own compute and storage costs. There is no official managed Harbor-as-a-service offering from the CNCF or the core project, though various cloud providers and vendors offer managed or supported Harbor deployments as commercial services built on top of the open source core.

Harbor is used primarily by platform engineering and DevOps teams at organizations that need a private, self-hosted container registry with enterprise-grade security controls, particularly in regulated industries such as finance and telecommunications where image provenance, vulnerability scanning and access control are compliance requirements rather than optional features. Publicly disclosed adopters include companies like Dynatrace, JD.com and OVHcloud, reflecting adoption both among large-scale internet companies and infrastructure providers.

Harbor competes with other container registry options including cloud-native managed registries like Amazon ECR, Google Artifact Registry and Azure Container Registry, as well as other self-hosted or open source registries like Docker Registry (Distribution), Quay and Nexus Repository. Its primary differentiation is combining a fully open source, vendor-neutral governance model under the CNCF with enterprise-grade security features, vulnerability scanning, image signing and role-based access control, that are often reserved for paid tiers in competing products. Because it requires self-hosting and operational management rather than being consumed as a fully managed SaaS from the project itself, Harbor tends to appeal most to organizations with existing Kubernetes and platform engineering expertise, while teams wanting a zero-ops managed registry more often default to their cloud provider's native offering instead.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Harbor is an open source, cloud-native container registry that stores, scans and signs container images and OCI artifacts, giving organizations a private, security-focused alternative to public registries like Docker Hub.

Originally built inside VMware's China R&D organization in 2014 and open sourced in 2016, Harbor joined the CNCF in 2018 and became a CNCF Graduated project in June 2020, the first open source container registry to reach that status.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Harbor integrates vulnerability scanners like Trivy or Clair to analyze stored images, and supports content trust and image signing through tools like Notary and Cosign so consumers can verify image provenance before deployment.

Its multi-tenant project model provides granular role-based access control integrated with LDAP, Active Directory and OIDC, alongside cross-registry replication, a built-in Helm chart repository, and retention and garbage collection policies.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Harbor is entirely free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license, with no paid tier or usage limit imposed by the project itself.

Organizations self-host Harbor on their own Kubernetes clusters or infrastructure and pay only their own compute and storage costs; some vendors offer managed or supported Harbor deployments as separate commercial services.

features.title: Vulnerability scanning
features.description: Integrates with scanners like Trivy or Clair to automatically analyze stored images for known vulnerabilities.

features.title: Content trust and image signing
features.description: Supports Notary and Cosign integration so consumers can verify an image's provenance and integrity before deployment.

features.title: Role-based access control
features.description: Granular, project-based access control that integrates with LDAP, Active Directory and OIDC identity providers.

features.title: Multi-tenancy with projects
features.description: Organizes images into isolated projects for different teams or applications.

features.title: Cross-registry replication
features.description: Mirrors images between multiple Harbor instances or across clouds for redundancy and distribution.

features.title: Helm chart repository
features.description: Built-in storage for Helm charts and other Kubernetes packaging artifacts.

features.title: Retention and garbage collection
features.description: Automated policies manage image retention and reclaim storage space.

features.title: Web UI and REST API
features.description: Full web-based management console alongside an API for CI/CD pipeline integration.

pros: CNCF Graduated project with strong governance and wide adoption
Completely free and open source under Apache 2.0
Enterprise-grade security scanning and image signing built in
Backed by contributions from more than 680 organizations
Deploys on any Kubernetes cluster or Docker-capable system
cons: Requires self-hosting and ongoing operational management
Initial setup and configuration can be complex for smaller teams
Can be resource-intensive at large scale
Upgrades between major versions require careful planning
Relies on external scanners like Trivy for vulnerability detection

faqs.question: What is Harbor?
faqs.answer: Harbor is an open source, CNCF-graduated container registry that stores, scans and signs container images and OCI artifacts with role-based access control for Kubernetes environments.

faqs.question: Is Harbor free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Harbor is entirely free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license, with organizations only paying for their own hosting infrastructure.

faqs.question: Who maintains Harbor?
faqs.answer: Harbor originated at VMware in 2014, was open sourced in 2016, and is now governed as a CNCF Graduated project with contributions from more than 680 organizations.

faqs.question: Can Harbor replace Docker Hub?
faqs.answer: Yes, many organizations use Harbor as a private, self-hosted alternative to public registries like Docker Hub, particularly when they need built-in vulnerability scanning and access control.

faqs.question: Does Harbor support Kubernetes natively?
faqs.answer: Yes, Harbor is designed to deploy on any Kubernetes environment via an official Helm chart, or on a system with Docker support.

faqs.question: What is Harbor's relationship to CNCF and VMware?
faqs.answer: Harbor was created at VMware in 2014, open sourced in 2016, and joined the Cloud Native Computing Foundation in 2018, graduating in June 2020 as the first open source container registry to reach that status.

use_cases.title: Private enterprise container registry
use_cases.description: Enterprises deploy Harbor as a secure, private registry for storing and distributing container images across Kubernetes clusters.
use_cases.audience: Platform engineering and DevOps teams

use_cases.title: Regulated industry compliance
use_cases.description: Organizations in finance, telecom and other regulated sectors use Harbor's scanning and signing features to meet image provenance and compliance requirements.
use_cases.audience: Regulated industries requiring image security compliance

use_cases.title: Internal developer platforms
use_cases.description: Platform teams build multi-tenant internal developer platforms using Harbor's project-based access control and replication features.
use_cases.audience: Platform teams building internal developer platforms
